Why are you interested in this role?: This role is L&D at its most strategic—owning the connective tissue between what a product ships and whether end users actually trust and adopt it. That's exactly the work I love. At InGenius Prep I design and maintain training curriculum across live, virtual, and self-serve formats and run a recurring update cadence, and the part I find most rewarding is making sure training keeps pace with change so nothing falls through the cracks. Doing that for clinicians—where adoption directly affects how well technology supports patient care—feels genuinely meaningful, and Abridge's evidence-first culture matches how I approach learning.
Tell us about your L&D / training progra: My approach to building training from scratch is: start with a needs assessment (1:1s to find the real gap), define success as measurable behavior change rather than activity, partner with SMEs who own the content while I own the pedagogy, deliver in layers (live + async + follow-up), and measure whether people actually use what they learned—then iterate. At InGenius I run that cycle for 20+ programs across formats; at Vestas I designed and piloted an Inclusive Leadership workshop that scaled to 23 global locations; and at NextGen I delivered internal training on DEI, Inclusive Leadership, and performance review. I'll be candid that I haven't trained clinicians specifically, but the system—diagnose, design, deliver, measure, iterate—transfers directly, and I learn a domain fast.
Tell us about a time training didn't lan: Early at InGenius I launched a new sales process with all the documentation neatly organized in a library, and I assumed adoption would follow. It didn't. So I went back and interviewed the reps and discovered the real problem wasn't resistance—it was friction; the materials were too hard to find in the moment. I rebuilt it as a single one-pager landing page that met people where they were, and adoption followed. My takeaway has stuck with me: assumption is the most expensive thing in L&D. It's exactly why I'd keep clinician modules current, easy to access, and validated against real engagement data rather than completion checkboxes.
How would you use data / AI in clinician: I'd track behavior, not just completion—are clinicians actually using features after training, and where do they drop off? At NextGen I analyzed 2,000+ employee responses to find what truly drives outcomes, and I'd bring that same rigor to LMS engagement and adoption metrics, reporting to PX leadership regularly. On AI specifically: I use AI tools daily and have been building a RAG-based learning system on the side, so I think about personalization concretely—learner personas by clinician role and care setting, just-in-time coaching, and scenario-based practice that meets people in their real workflow.
Why Abridge?: I'm drawn to Abridge because you treat trust as something to be earned through evidence—mapping AI output to ground truth so clinicians can verify it—and that empirical mindset is exactly how I approach learning design. My graduate training in Applied Organizational Psychology taught me to treat training as research: study where people struggle, design solutions that stick, and measure whether they actually changed behavior. I'd be excited to own a best-in-class training experience that helps your technology genuinely improve clinical workflows.
